topic_name: Parallel Operation of Transformers - ESE Prelims (1980-2026)

questions.question: Which of the following conditions is most essential for the parallel operation of two 1-phase transformers?
questions.explanation: Incorrect polarity results in the two secondary voltages adding up in the local loop, causing a catastrophic short circuit[cite: 1].

questions.question: Two transformers are connected in parallel. These transformers share the load in proportion to their kVA ratings if their:
questions.explanation: For load sharing to be proportional to ratings, the per-unit impedances on their own respective kVA bases must be identical[cite: 1].

questions.question: In case of parallel operation of three-phase transformers, which of the following vector groups can be paralleled together?
questions.explanation: Transformers belonging to the same group or having the same phase displacement (e.g., Yy0 and Dd0) can be paralleled[cite: 1].

questions.question: For two parallel transformers, if the $X/R$ ratios are not equal, then:

options.b: The total power factor will be improved
options.c: They will operate at different power factors
options.d: The secondary voltages will be out of phase by 180 degrees

questions.explanation: Different X/R ratios cause the current phasors to be displaced from each other, resulting in different operating power factors for each unit[cite: 1].

questions.question: A circulating current is produced in a parallel transformer bank when there is a mismatch in:
questions.explanation: Unequal voltage ratios create a net EMF in the secondary loop, driving a circulating current even at no-load[cite: 1].

questions.question: What is the primary consequence of connecting two transformers in parallel with different phase sequences?
questions.explanation: Mismatched phase sequences result in very high potential differences between corresponding phases, causing a short circuit[cite: 1].

questions.question: Two transformers A and B are in parallel. If transformer A has a lower per-unit impedance than B, then:

options.a: A will be overloaded compared to B
options.b: B will be overloaded compared to A
options.d: The total capacity will be the sum of their ratings

questions.explanation: Load sharing is inversely proportional to impedance. The unit with lower impedance will draw more current and reach its rating first[cite: 1].

questions.question: For two 3-phase transformers to operate in parallel, they must have identical:
questions.explanation: To ensure secondary voltages are in phase and avoid short circuits, both phase displacement (vector group) and phase sequence must match[cite: 1].

questions.question: Two transformers A and B are connected in parallel. Transformer A has a rating of 500 kVA and 5% impedance, while B has a rating of 250 kVA and 5% impedance. The total load they can share without overloading either is:
questions.explanation: Since their per-unit impedances are equal on their own ratings, they share the load exactly in proportion to their ratings ($500 + 250 = 750$ kVA)[cite: 1].

questions.question: If two transformers with different $X/R$ ratios are operated in parallel, the current shared by them will be:

options.a: In phase with the total load current
options.b: Out of phase with each other
options.c: In phase with each other but out of phase with the load current
options.d: Exactly equal to the half of total load current

questions.explanation: Mismatched $X/R$ ratios cause a phase displacement between the transformer current phasors, leading to different operating power factors[cite: 1].

questions.question: Two 1-phase transformers with equal voltage ratios and ratings of 250 kVA each are in parallel. $Z_A = 1+j4$ ohms and $Z_B = 2+j6$ ohms. If the total load is 500 kVA, transformer A will be:
questions.explanation: Load sharing is inversely proportional to impedance magnitude. Since $|Z_A| < |Z_B|$, transformer A will take more than half the load and thus exceed its 250 kVA rating[cite: 1].

questions.question: In parallel operation, the circulating current is given by $(E_1 - E_2) / (Z_1 + Z_2)$. This current flows:

options.b: Only in the local loop of the two secondaries
options.d: Between the core and the tank

questions.explanation: Circulating current due to voltage mismatch exists even at no-load and circulates within the secondary windings of the parallel units[cite: 1].

questions.question: A 1000 kVA transformer with 4% reactance is paralleled with a 500 kVA transformer with 3% reactance. On a 1000 kVA base, the equivalent reactance of the combination is:
A transformer with vector group Yy0 is to be paralleled with another unit. Which of the following vector groups is compatible for this operation?
questions.explanation: Transformers must have the same phase displacement to be paralleled. Both Yy0 and Dd0 belong to Group 1 with 0° phase shift.

questions.question: Two 3-phase transformers belonging to Group 3 (phase shift -30°) and Group 4 (phase shift +30°) are connected in parallel. The resulting phase difference between their secondary voltages is:
questions.explanation: The relative phase shift is the difference between +30° and -30°, which is 60°. This will cause massive circulating currents.

questions.question: In the context of parallel operation, 'Polarity Test' is performed to ensure:

options.b: That the secondary EMFs oppose each other in the local loop

questions.explanation: Correct polarity ensures that the secondary voltages are in opposition within the local loop, resulting in zero net voltage at no-load.

questions.question: If two transformers in parallel have different $X/R$ ratios, the transformer with the higher $R/X$ ratio will operate at a power factor that is:

options.a: Higher than the load power factor
options.b: Lower than the load power factor
options.c: Equal to the load power factor

questions.explanation: The unit with the higher resistance-to-reactance ratio (more resistive) will operate at a leading power factor relative to the load current.

questions.question: When paralleling a Delta-Star (Dy1) transformer with a Star-Delta (Yd1) transformer, the total phase shift difference is:
questions.explanation: Dy1 (-30°) and Yd1 (-30°) actually have the same displacement and can be paralleled if their phase sequences and ratios match.

questions.question: Two transformers with leakage impedances Z1 and Z2 are connected in parallel. The load sharing will be proportional to their ratings if:

options.b: Per-unit impedances on a common base are equal
options.c: Per-unit impedances on their own bases are equal

questions.explanation: Equal per-unit impedances on their respective ratings ensure the units are loaded to the same percentage of their capacity.

questions.question: Two auto-transformers A and B with equal ratings are in parallel. If $Z_A = 0.02 + j0.05$ pu and $Z_B = 0.04 + j0.10$ pu, which transformer will take more load?

options.d: Depends on the load power factor

questions.explanation: Load sharing is inversely proportional to impedance magnitude. Since $|Z_A| < |Z_B|$, Transformer A takes more load.

questions.question: In parallel operation of auto-transformers, if the $X/R$ ratios are different, then:

options.a: The transformers operate at the same power factor
options.b: The transformers operate at different power factors
options.c: The total load is shared equally regardless of impedance

questions.explanation: Different X/R ratios cause the current phasors to be displaced, resulting in different operating power factors for each unit.

questions.question: Two transformers are connected in parallel to supply a common load. The load shared by each transformer is inversely proportional to its:
questions.explanation: Current division in parallel circuits is governed by the inverse of the branch impedances.

questions.question: A circulating current in parallel transformers is primarily caused by a mismatch in:
questions.explanation: Unequal voltage ratios create a potential difference that drives a circulating current even at no-load.

questions.question: Two single-phase transformers A and B are connected in parallel. Their leakage impedances are $Z_A = (0.1 + j0.4)\ \Omega$ and $Z_B = (0.05 + j0.2)\ \Omega$. The load shared by A as a fraction of the total load is:
questions.explanation: The ratio of load shared is $S_A / S_{total} = Z_B / (Z_A + Z_B)$. Since $Z_A = 2 	imes Z_B$, $S_A$ will be $1/3$ of the total load.

questions.question: Three-phase transformers belonging to which vector groups can be operated in parallel?
questions.explanation: Only transformers with the same phase displacement (e.g., both 0° or both 30°) can be paralleled.

questions.question: Two 100 kVA single-phase transformers with equal voltage ratios have per-unit leakage impedances of $0.01 + j0.05$ and $0.02 + j0.10$ on their own ratings. When operating in parallel, the total load they can take without overloading either is:
questions.explanation: The transformer with the lower impedance ($Z_1$) will reach its full capacity first. Since $Z_2 = 2 \times Z_1$, when $S_1 = 100\ \text{kVA}$, $S_2$ will only be $50\ \text{kVA}$. Total load = $100 + 50 = 150\ \text{kVA}$.

questions.question: If the phase sequence of one of the two three-phase transformers connected in parallel is reversed, the result will be:

options.b: The transformers will operate at different power factors
options.c: A dead short circuit will occur

questions.explanation: Incorrect phase sequence leads to massive potential differences between terminals, causing a catastrophic short circuit.

questions.question: Two transformers with $Z_{pu1} = 0.05$ and $Z_{pu2} = 0.08$ on a common base are in parallel. If the total load is $S_L$, the load shared by transformer 1 ($S_1$) is given by:
questions.explanation: Load shared is inversely proportional to impedance: $S_1 = S_L \times [Z_2 / (Z_1 + Z_2)]$. Substituting gives $S_L \times (0.08 / 0.13)$.

questions.question: In parallel operation, the condition for sharing load in proportion to kVA ratings is that their per-unit impedances must be equal on:
questions.explanation: When $Z_{pu}$ on their own bases are equal, the percentage loading on each transformer remains the same.

questions.question: Two transformers A and B are in parallel with $E_A = 230\ V$ and $E_B = 220\ V$. If the impedances are $Z_A = (0.5 + j2)\ \Omega$ and $Z_B = (0.5 + j2)\ \Omega$, the circulating current at no-load is:
Two transformers with $X/R$ ratios of 3 and 10 respectively are connected in parallel. If the total load power factor is 0.8 lagging, then:

options.b: The one with $X/R = 10$ operates at a higher pf
options.c: The one with $X/R = 3$ operates at a higher pf

questions.explanation: A lower $X/R$ ratio means the transformer is more resistive. The more resistive unit always operates at a power factor higher (closer to unity) than the more reactive unit.

questions.question: Two single-phase transformers have the same voltage ratio and kVA ratings. However, their per-unit leakage impedances are $Z_1 = 0.04 + j0.06$ and $Z_2 = 0.04 + j0.08$. In parallel, the maximum common load they can supply without overloading either is:

options.a: The sum of their kVA ratings
options.b: Less than the sum of their kVA ratings
options.c: More than the sum of their kVA ratings

questions.explanation: Because their impedance magnitudes are different, the transformer with the lower impedance ($Z_1$) will reach its full capacity while the other is still underloaded. Therefore, the total system capacity is less than the arithmetic sum of their individual ratings.

questions.question: Two transformers $T_1$ and $T_2$ are connected in parallel. $T_1$ has a reactance of 0.1 pu on its own base of 100 MVA. $T_2$ has a reactance of 0.1 pu on its own base of 50 MVA. The load shared by $T_1$ and $T_2$ will be in the ratio of:
questions.explanation: Since their pu impedances are equal on their own bases, they share load in proportion to their ratings ($100:50$), which simplifies to $2:1$.

questions.question: For two 3-phase transformers to be paralleled, which of the following is a 'desirable' but not 'mandatory' condition?
questions.explanation: Mismatched $X/R$ ratios only lead to different operating power factors (less efficient sharing), whereas mismatched sequence, ratio, or displacement cause dangerous circulating currents or short circuits.

questions.question: The percentage impedance of a 100 kVA transformer is 4%. If it is connected in parallel with a 200 kVA transformer of 4% impedance, the combined percentage impedance on a 100 kVA base is:
questions.explanation: On a 100 kVA base, $Z_1 = 0.04$. $Z_2$ on a 100 kVA base is $0.04 \times (100/200) = 0.02$. The parallel combination is $(0.04 \times 0.02) / (0.04 + 0.02) = 0.0133$ or 1.33%.

questions.question: Two three-phase transformers A and B are to be connected in parallel. Transformer A has a vector group of Dy11. Which of the following vector groups is compatible for parallel operation with transformer A?
questions.explanation: To avoid phase shift conflicts, transformers must belong to the same vector group or have identical phase displacement. Both Dy11 and Yd11 provide a +30° phase shift.

questions.question: Two transformers with per-unit impedances $Z_1 = 0.01 + j0.04$ and $Z_2 = 0.01 + j0.04$ are paralleled. If the voltage ratio of transformer 1 is 5% higher than transformer 2, the circulating current at no-load is limited by:

options.a: The sum of their leakage impedances
options.b: The difference of their leakage impedances

questions.explanation: The circulating current $I_c$ is driven by the voltage difference and is limited by the series combination of the two leakage impedances ($Z_1 + Z_2$).

questions.question: A 500 kVA transformer with 5% reactance is connected in parallel with a 250 kVA transformer with 4% reactance. The total load is 750 kVA. The load shared by the 250 kVA transformer is:
questions.explanation: On a 500 kVA base: $X_1 = 0.05$ and $X_2 = 0.04 \times (500/250) = 0.08$. Load shared by $T_2 = 750 \times [X_1 / (X_1 + X_2)] = 750 \times (0.05 / 0.13) \approx 288.46$ kVA. Correcting for base: $S_2 = S_L \times [ (S_2/X_{pu2}) / (S_1/X_{pu1} + S_2/X_{pu2}) ] = 750 \times [ (250/0.04) / (500/0.05 + 250/0.04) ] = 750 \times (6250 / 16250) \approx 288.46$ kVA. The closest standardized GATE option is typically derived from $S_2 = S_L \times [Z_1 / (Z_1 + Z_2)]$ using common base values.

questions.question: If two parallel transformers have different voltage ratios, they can still be operated in parallel provided that:

options.a: The circulating current does not exceed the rated current
options.c: They have the same kVA rating
options.d: They have the same cooling system

questions.explanation: While identical ratios are ideal, small differences are permissible as long as the resulting circulating current does not cause overheating or exceed the thermal limits of the windings.

questions.question: Two 1-phase transformers are connected in parallel. If their $X/R$ ratios are 5 and 2 respectively, the transformer with $X/R = 5$ will carry a current that:

options.c: Is in phase with the total load current

questions.explanation: A higher $X/R$ ratio indicates a more inductive unit. Therefore, its current will lag further behind the voltage than the current of the more resistive unit ($X/R = 2$).

questions.question: Two transformers A and B are connected in parallel. If their $X/R$ ratios are not the same, they will operate at:
questions.explanation: Different $X/R$ ratios cause a phase shift between the internal impedance triangles of the transformers, forcing them to operate at different power factors relative to each other.

questions.question: For the parallel operation of two 3-phase transformers, it is 'essential' that they have the same:
questions.explanation: Incorrect phase sequence leads to a huge potential difference between the busbars, causing a massive short circuit.

questions.question: Which of the following vector groups of 3-phase transformers can be paralleled together?
questions.explanation: Transformers must have the same phase displacement (0° in this case) to be connected in parallel without circulating currents.

questions.question: If two transformers with unequal voltage ratios are operated in parallel, then:

options.a: One will be overloaded and the other underloaded
options.c: One will act as a motor

questions.explanation: Unequal ratios create a circulating current which adds to the load current in one unit and subtracts from the other, potentially overloading one transformer.

questions.question: In parallel operation, load sharing is proportional to kVA ratings if:

options.b: Per-unit impedances on a common base are equal
options.c: Per-unit impedances on their own bases are equal

questions.explanation: Identical per-unit impedance on their respective ratings ensures they are loaded to the same percentage of their full capacity.

questions.question: Two transformers operate in parallel. If they have different percentage impedances, how will they share the load?

options.a: The transformer with the higher impedance takes more load
options.b: The transformer with the lower impedance takes more load
options.d: Load sharing is independent of impedance

questions.explanation: Load shared by a transformer is inversely proportional to its percentage impedance. Therefore, the unit with the lower impedance magnitude will be more heavily loaded.

questions.question: Which of the following results in a permanent circulating current in two transformers connected in parallel?
questions.explanation: A mismatch in voltage ratios creates a resultant EMF in the local loop of the two secondaries, which drives a circulating current even at no-load.

questions.question: A Star-Star (Yy0) transformer can be successfully paralleled with which of the following?
questions.explanation: Parallel operation requires identical phase displacement. Both Yy0 and Dd0 belong to Group 1, which has a 0° phase shift.

questions.question: What is the consequence of paralleling two transformers with different $X/R$ ratios?

options.a: One transformer will operate at a better power factor than the other
options.b: Both will operate at the same power factor

questions.explanation: Different $X/R$ ratios cause a phase displacement between the current phasors of the two transformers. This results in each transformer operating at a different power factor.

questions.question: For the parallel operation of three-phase transformers, the phase sequence must be:
questions.explanation: Identical phase sequence is an essential condition. An incorrect sequence leads to a massive potential difference and a short circuit.
